title: Memories of My Melancholy Whores
description: Memories of My Melancholy Whores is a novella by Gabriel García Márquez. translated into Persian by Kaveh Mirabasi
descriptionParagraphs: Memories of My Melancholy Whores is a novella by Gabriel García Márquez. translated into Persian by Kaveh Mirabasi

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a novella, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Iran. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: translated into Persian by Kaveh Mirabasi More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Memories of My Melancholy Whores is a novella by Gabriel García Márquez. translated into Persian by Kaveh Mirabasi
banningParagraphs: Memories of My Melancholy Whores entered censorship debates as a novella associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is Post-1979 Islamic Republic period in Iran, where Iranian censors and the Ministry of Culture and Islamic Guidance banned or denied publication. translated into Persian by Kaveh Mirabasi translated into Persian by Kaveh Mirabasi

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
